segments.s061-g57: نفس را places 'breath' as a definite object with the postposition/marker را; the clause uses مگر ... نیست to express an emphatic negation: 'there is no way for the breath (to reach)'. بر لبش is a prepositional phrase 'onto her lip' (بر + لب + ـش). راه نیست is the copular negative 'there is no way'.
segments.s061-g58: چنو (classical variant of چنان) introduces a comparison/degree 'such (that)'; در جهان نیز 'in the world likewise/indeed' and یک ماه نیست is a simple negated existential: 'there is not one moon' — i.e. 'there is no other moon/beauty like her.' The clause functions as the reason/explanation for the previous negation.
segments.s061-g59: پرستندگان 'the worshippers/adorers' is the subject; هر یکی (literally 'each one') distributes the action to individuals; آشکار here is adverbial 'openly/manifestly' describing how they acted.
segments.s061-g60: همی کرد uses the classical progressive/iterative marker همی + past-root کرد to mean 'would do/kept on doing' (habitual past). وصف رخ آن نگار is the direct object 'the description of the face of that beloved' (وصف = description; رخ = face; آن نگار = that beloved).
segments.s061-g61: بدین چاره is a prepositional phrase 'by/with this expedient' (بدین = 'with this'); تا introduces purpose here ('so that/in order that'); آن لب لعل فام is the goal phrase 'that ruby‑colored lip' (لعل فام = ruby‑hued).
segments.s061-g62: کند is the present/subjunctive form 'may/might make' (used here in the purposive result clause); آشنا with کند means 'to make (someone) acquainted' or 'to bring into intimacy/meeting'; با لب پور سام marks 'with the lip of Pour‑Sam' (با = with, پور سام is the patronymic/name).
segments.s061-g63: چنین گفت 'thus said' introduces reported speech or a remark; با بندگان خوب‌چهر is the dative/indirect object 'to (his) fair‑faced servants'. (Note: the provided sense list for گفت did not include the verbal sense 'to say', so that item is marked accordingly below.)
segments.s061-g64: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'; با ماه خوبست is a predicative phrase 'is pleasing/beautiful with the moon' or 'shines with the moon'; رخشنده مهر is the subject/predicand 'radiant affection' (رخشنده = radiant, مهر = love/affection). Overall this clause states that love/kindness shines in conjunction with moon‑like beauty.
segments.s061-g161: خرامد is a third-person singular past/narrative verb (literary) meaning “strode” or “marched proudly”; the verb is placed before its subject here because of poetic inversion. مگر is a small particle/modal word inserted parenthetically (poetic/concessive nuance, here best read as a slight contrastive/illustrative 'as/indeed' rather than a literal 'unless'). پهلوان is the subject ‘the hero/champion’. با + کمند is an instrumental prepositional phrase ‘with a lasso’ (با marks the instrument).
segments.s061-g162: به نزدیک is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning ‘near/at the approach of’; به here marks direction/position. دیوار کاخ بلند is a noun phrase with a genitive relationship (دیوارِ کاخِ بلند — 'the wall of the high palace'), where کاخ (palace) modifies دیوار and بلند (tall/high) is a post‑nominal adjective modifying کاخ; Persian omits the written ezāfe in many editions but the grammatical linking is genitive/attributive.
segments.s061-g163: کند is a literary third‑person singular verb here meaning ‘he made/put’ (past); the subject is understood from context (the hero). حلقه در گردن کنگره is the object phrase: حلقه 'a loop/ring' + در گردن 'around/on the neck of' + کنگره 'the battlement/merlon' — together ‘he put a loop around the neck (top) of the battlement’. Note the noun‑of‑noun link (گردنِ کنگره) is expressed without an explicit ezāfe in the text.
segments.s061-g164: شود is a third‑person singular (present/future or resultative) verbal form meaning ‘becomes’ or ‘is made’; شیر شاد is a simple nominal phrase (شیر 'the lion' + postposed adjective شاد 'glad'). از شکار بره is a causal/ablative phrase 'from/because of the capture (شکار) of the lamb (بره)' where از marks the cause or source and شکار is 'prey/capture'.
segments.s061-g165: برفتند is a literary third‑person plural past tense 'they went/departed'. خوبان is the plural noun 'the good/beautiful ones' (companions or beloveds) serving as the subject of برفتند. و links the clauses; برگشت is a third‑person singular past 'returned' and زال (proper name) follows as its subject — a common poetic verb‑before‑subject order.
segments.s061-g166: دلش is a possessive phrase (دل + enclitic ش = 'his heart'). گشت is a past/resultative verb 'became' or 'turned into'. با کام و شادی is an instrumental/causal phrase 'with desire (کام) and joy (شادی)', indicating the manner or content of his heart's change. همال functions as a predicate/qualifier (literary 'bearer' or 'fellow‑bearer' in a figurative sense) describing the state into which his heart turned; here it reads as a figurative epithet rather than a literal porter.
segments.s061-g9: همی is a frequentive/progressive adverb here ('kept/was'), گل is the noun 'flowers', چدند is an archaic/poetic form (here to be read as چیدند 'they picked' or possibly چندند 'how many there were') and functions as the main verbal idea, and از لب رودبار is a prepositional phrase 'from/along the lip (bank) of the river' (ز/از = from, لب = bank/brink, رودبار = river‑valley/riverbank). Word order is poetic (adverb + object + verb + source phrase).
segments.s061-g10: رخان is used adjectivally/nominally here describing the people (poetic use; see note below), چون is the simile 'like', گلستان is 'rose‑garden', and گل در کنار ('gol dar kenar') is a locative phrase 'flowers at their side' (در کنار = beside/at the edge). The sequence places the simile immediately after the described noun as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s061-g11: نگه کرد is a compound verbal phrase: نگه (gaze/watch or 'lift/hold' in poetic usage) plus کرد (past 'did/made') yielding 'he looked/held/raised (his) ...'. دستان is the noun 'hands' (here indicating a gesture), and ز تخت بلند is a prepositional phrase 'from the high/lofty throne' (ز = from, تخت = throne, بلند = lofty). The clause order is verb + object + source phrase, a flexible classical order.
segments.s061-g12: بپرسید is the past tense 3rd‑person form 'he asked' (classical narrative use). کاین is a demonstrative 'this/that (which)', گل پرستان is a compound noun 'flower‑worshippers' (گل = flower, پرستان = worshippers/devotees), کی is the interrogative 'who', and ند here is an archaic/copular form representing the 3rd‑person plural copula (classical spelling of اند). The clause therefore reports his question about the identity of those devotees.
segments.s061-g13: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (classical past), گوینده is the agent 'the speaker/narrator', and با پهلوان means 'to/with the hero' (با used as the preposition marking the addressee). The clause order is adverb + verb + subject + adpositional phrase—typical of epic diction.
segments.s061-g14: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'who') giving content of what was said. از کاخ identifies origin 'from the palace', مهراب is a proper name here (Mehrab) rather than the architectural 'mihrab' (classical verse often uses names without overt markers), and روشن‌روان is an epithet 'bright‑souled' describing the person in that palace. The whole phrase is a relative/prepositional fragment continuing into the next sentence.
segments.s061-g15: پرستندگان is the plural direct object 'the worshippers/devotees' and را is the definite object marker marking them as what is being sent; سوی گلستان is the directional phrase 'toward the rose‑garden' (سوی = toward, گلستان = rose garden).
segments.s061-g16: فرستد is the classical past 3rd‑person 'sent' (narrative past used in epic), همی is the habitual/progressive adverb 'continually/was', and ماه کابلستان is a kenning/title 'the moon (i.e., the beautiful one) of Kabulistan' functioning as the subject who dispatches the worshippers. The order is verb + adverb + subject (poetic inversion).
segments.s061-g105: This is a verb‑first poetic clause: بیامد (past tense 'came') appears before the noun phrase سیه‌چشم گنجور شاه, which is a stacked epithet ('dark‑eyed' + 'treasure‑laden') modifying شاه. Persian poetry often places the verb first for rhythm; the whole segment names the arriving figure.
segments.s061-g106: که introduces a relative clause referring back to the king: 'who was...'. بود is the past copula 'was', اندر آن کار is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in that matter', and دستور شاه is the predicate in genitive: 'the king's دستور' (here 'organizer/one who gives orders'). Word order is Subject (implied 'who') + بود + adverbial + predicate.
segments.s061-g107: سخن ('words, speech') is the theme; هر چه بشنید is a quantifying relative phrase 'whatever he heard' (هر + چه + past verb). از آن دلنواز is a source phrase 'from that charming one'. The clause order is noun + quantifier phrase + verb + source.
segments.s061-g108: همی + past verb here conveys iterative/continuous past: 'he kept saying' or 'he told'. گفت is used as a verbal past (poetic form). پیش سپهبد ('in the presence of the spahbed') is a locative phrase, and به راز ('in secret'/'confidentially') is an adverbial indicating manner — together: 'he confided to the spahbed.'
segments.s061-g109: سپهبد is the subject and خرامید is the past tense verb meaning 'strode/proudly walked'. تا گلستان here indicates spatial extent/destination 'as far as / to the rose‑garden' (تا used for direction/extent rather than temporal 'until').
segments.s061-g110: بر امید is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in hope of' (بر + noun), and خورشید کابلستان is a genitive noun phrase 'the Sun of Kabulistan' — an honorific/epithet for the expected person. The whole phrase gives the motive for his movement.
segments.s061-g111: This is a compact adjectival/noun string describing the women: پری‌روی ('fairy‑faced'), گلرخ ('rose‑faced/rosy‑cheeked'), بتان‌طراز ('idol‑fashioned' — i.e. beautifully made like idols). Persian poetry commonly stacks epithets without linking verbs to paint a picture.
segments.s061-g112: Two coordinated past verbs: برفتند 'they advanced/went forward' and بردند 'they carried/brought'. پیشش is 'before him/in his presence' and نماز here means ritual salutation or formal homage (in epic usage 'نماز بردن' often = to pay respects). The sequence shows action (approach) plus purpose (presenting salutations).
segments.s061-g1: پرستنده (subject) + برخاست (past verb) + از پیش اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from before him / out of his presence.' برخاست is past tense and here carries the sense of rising/departing; اوی is an archaic/pronounced form of the third‑person pronoun 'او' ('him').
segments.s061-g2: بدان functions as 'to/unto that' (a target demonstrative phrase) and introduces the object 'چاره بی‌چاره' ('that helpless remedy'). بنهاد is the past of نهادن 'to place / set' used here in the sense 'turned/laid (one's) face toward'; روی is the noun 'face.' Word order is noun phrase then verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s061-g3: به + noun phrase marks the manner/material: 'in/with Byzantine brocade.' بیاراستند is the perfect/past plural of آراستن 'to adorn/dress,' so the clause means 'they adorned [her] in Rûm (Byzantine) brocade.'
segments.s061-g4: سر زلف is a coordinated noun phrase 'head and (locks of) hair.' برگل is an adjectival/compound element meaning 'with flowers / flower‑adorned.' بپیراستند is again the past plural of آراستن, placed after the objects in typical poetic order: 'they adorned the head and locks with flowers.'
segments.s061-g5: برفتند is past plural 'they went / departed.' هر پنج تا is a numeral phrase 'all five' (تا is a colloquial/counting particle here), and رودبار is the place 'river‑valley' or 'riverside' serving as the object/locative. The verb precedes the phrase in this poetic line.
segments.s061-g6: ز هر بوی و رنگی is an ablative phrase 'from every scent and color' (z = 'from'). چو introduces a simile 'like,' and خرم بهار 'the joyful/lush spring' completes the comparison; the clause describes that they (or the place) had every scent and hue resembling spring.
segments.s061-g7: مه فرودین reads 'the moon/month Farvardin' (مه here = month/moon) and وسر سال بود 'and the head/start of the year it was.' بود is the past copula 'was.' The phrase states the time: Farvardin, the beginning of the year.
segments.s061-g8: لب رود is a locative 'on the river's brink/edge.' لشکرگه زال is 'Zāl's army‑camp' (the genitive relationship is implied without an explicit ezāfe in the verse), and بود is the copula 'was'—so the clause states that Zāl's camp was on the riverbank.
segments.s061-g17: به (preposition) + نزد (prepositional noun) = 'to/in the presence of'; پری چهرگان is a compound attributive phrase ('pārī' = fairy, 'chahargān' plural of face/appearance) modifying the locus. رفت is a past tense verb 'went'; the subject زال appears after the verb (verb–subject order), a poetic inversion common in Ferdowsi for rhythm and emphasis.
segments.s061-g18: کمان (the object 'bow') is fronted, followed by خواست (past 'desired/asked for') and the source phrase از ترک ('from the Turk'); the subject is omitted here and is carried over from the previous clause (Zāl). و links the next verb بفراخت ('roused/raised') which takes یال ('mane/crest') as its object. Note the verb–object order and omission of an explicit subject — typical poetic compression.
segments.s061-g19: پیاده is an adverbial/attribute 'on foot'; همی + رفت is the classical progressive/past construction ('kept/was going') with رفت as the past verb. جویان شکار is the subject phrase 'seekers of prey' (i.e. hunters) placed after the verb (another poetic inversion). شکار here names the quarry and forms a noun phrase with جویان ('those who seek').
segments.s061-g20: خشیشار is a proper name (the perceiver). دید is simple past 'saw/beheld'. اندر آن رودبار is a locative prepositional phrase ('in/within that river‑valley'), with اندر = 'in', آن = 'that', رودبار = 'river‑valley'.
segments.s061-g21: کمان + ترک گلرخ is a genitive/attributive chain 'the bow of the Turk Golrukh' (گلرخ naming the Turk). به زه بر نهاد is a verbal phrase meaning 'he placed/put (it) upon the string' — زه = bowstring, بر نهاد = 'placed upon' (نهاد past of نهادن plus the preposition بر). The verb نهاد is used in the concrete sense 'placed/put'.
segments.s061-g22: به دست X در نهاد is a secondary clause continuing the action: به دست = 'into the hand (of)', جهان‌پهلوان = the addressee/recipient 'world‑champion', در نهاد repeats the verb نهادن ('he placed/put in'). The construction places the previously mentioned object (the strung bow) into the hand of the hero; subject is again omitted and understood from context.
segments.s061-g23: نگه (nominal root 'gaze/watch') + کرد forms a verb 'kept watch/observed' (کرد is the light verb). تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'until', followed by مرغ برخاست ز آب: مرغ (subject 'the bird') + برخاست (past 'rose') + ز (poetic 'from') + آب ('water'). The clause expresses waiting until the bird rose out of the water.
segments.s061-g24: یکی 'one' (here 'one [of them]' or 'someone/a single person') is the actor; تیره 'dark' qualifies the object (an implicitly thrown missile/shaft), and بنداخت is past 'cast/hurled'. اندر شتاب is a locative/temporal phrase 'in haste' (اندر = 'in', شتاب = 'haste'). The object is elliptical (understood from context) — the clause means 'one hurled a dark projectile in haste.'
segments.s061-g41: شه نیمروزست is an equational clause: شه (Shah/king) + نیمروزست (نیمروز + است, an archaic/poetic fusion meaning “is Nimruz” or literally “is midday”) — together: “he is Shah Nimruz.” فرزند سام follows as a nominal apposition ‘son of Sam’ (Persian places the apposition after the name).
segments.s061-g42: که introduces a relative clause ‘whose…’; دستانش uses دستان = ‘tales/exploits’ with the possessive enclitic -ش. خوانند is a present-tense, 3rd-person plural classical form meaning ‘they call’ (not ‘recite’ here). شاهان is the agent ‘the kings.’ به نام is an idiom ‘by name / in name’ (i.e. they call them so). The clause: ‘whose tales/exploits the kings call by name.’
segments.s061-g43: This is an address/imperative-colloquial turn: بگرد is a hortative/imperative ‘let him go/roam’ (classical Persian uses ب‑ + verb). جهان‌گر is a vocative or descriptive noun ‘one who loves/roams the world’ (i.e. a world-rover). بگردد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive ‘may he roam / if he roams,’ and سوار is the logical subject ‘the rider/horseman.’ Word order is emphatic/poetic: the verb forms frame the noun phrases.
segments.s061-g44: ازین سان is a frozen phrase ‘in this manner / like this’ (از + این + سان). نبیند is negative present/subjunctive (classical form) meaning ‘he will not see / one does not see.’ یکی functions as an indefinite ‘anyone / anyone one’ and نامدار is an adjective ‘renowned, celebrated.’ The clause says that a rider roaming the world will not see anyone so famed.
segments.s061-g45: پرستنده is an agent noun ‘admirer/devotee’ (someone showing devotion or admiration). با marks accompaniment ‘with,’ and کودک ماه روی is a noun phrase where ماه روی is a compound adjective ‘moon‑faced’ (beautiful-faced child). The whole segment: ‘the admirer, with the moon-faced child…’
segments.s061-g46: بخندید is a past tense 3rd-person singular ‘he laughed/smiled.’ و connects clauses. گفتش is ‘he said to him/her’ (گفت + enclitic ـش = to him/her). که introduces the quoted injunction and چندین مگوی is a negative imperative: چندین ‘many/several’ + مگوی (مگو = negative imperative ‘do not say’, here with final -ی as a phonetic ending). Literally: ‘he laughed and said to him, “do not say many (things).”’
segments.s061-g47: که begins an explanatory clause ‘for/that.’ ماهیست is the fused form of ماه است ‘(she) is a moon’ (poetic predication: someone is likened to the moon). مهراب here functions as the proper name 'Mehrab' (though in the provided senses it also can mean mihrab/niche); را is the object/definite marker but in older idiom can mark possession/association (Mehrab has a moon). در سرای ‘in the house/palace’ locates that moon. The clause therefore means roughly ‘for Mehrab has a moon in his house.’
segments.s061-g48: به یک سر is an idiomatic measure ‘by one head/one degree’ (به + یک سر = ‘to the extent of one head’). ز here is the classical preposition ‘than / from’ in comparisons. شاه تو means ‘your king’ (شاه + possessive تو). برتر is the comparative adjective ‘superior.’ بپای is an older verbal form meaning roughly ‘stands/exists (in that relation)’ — together the phrase says ‘in one respect she is superior to your king.’ Word order is inverted for poetic emphasis.
segments.s061-g113: سپهبد (spahbed) is the subject; بپرسید is a past/remote form meaning 'asked' (here reported past). ازیشان is the contracted poetic sequence of از + ایشان meaning 'of/about them' and links the verb to the object; سخن is the noun 'speech/words' as the direct object of بپرسید.
segments.s061-g114: ز بالا ('z bālā') uses the poetic preposition ز = از 'from/of' plus بالا 'above/high' to mark source or vantage ('from above'); و is the coordinating 'and'; دیدار here means 'sight/seeing' or 'meeting' and takes آن سرو بن ('that cypress's base') as its object, with آن 'that' and سرو 'cypress' modified by بن meaning the base/root of the tree.
segments.s061-g115: ز here again is the poetic preposition 'about/from'; a series of nouns is coordinated by و: گفتار 'speech', دیدار 'meeting/sight', رای 'counsel/opinion' and خرد 'wisdom'. The list is a set of topics the questioner is to inquire into.
segments.s061-g116: بدان is composed of ب + دان (ب 'to/into' + دان 'to know/consider') and functions here as 'attend to that / consider that'; تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that' (تا = so that); به خوی وی اندر خورد is a verbal phrase where به خوی وی = 'into his disposition/character' (به = into/toward, خوی وی = his nature) and اندر خورد ('enter/come into') is a poetic compound meaning 'to enter into' or 'become part of'.
segments.s061-g117: بگویید is an imperative/plural polite form 'speak/say' (addressing the group); با من 'with me' marks the addressee; یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each and every'; سخن is the noun 'word/speech' — together: 'tell each word to me'.
segments.s061-g118: به is the preposition 'toward/regarding'; کژی 'crookedness/wrongness' names the wrongful attitude to be avoided; نگر is an imperative 'do not regard/look upon (as)...' in its prohibitive sense here (classically used as a negative command when followed by نفگنید); نفگنید is a negative imperative 'do not utter/breathe' (lit. 'do not blow/breathe'), ایچ means 'nothing/not at all' and بن functions emphatically in poetry — together: 'do not look on them as crooked; do not even speak a single thing of it.'
segments.s061-g119: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; راستی‌تان is 'your truthfulness / honesty' with the plural/formal enclitic تان; بود is the past copula 'were/was' used here in the protasis to express a counterfactual/conditional; گفت‌وگوی is the noun 'conversation' (the topic/result implied by the condition). The clause sets up: 'If your honesty were (so), [then] the conversation...'.
segments.s061-g120: به نزدیک من is a locative phrase 'in my presence / near me' (به + نزدیک + من); تان is the possessive plural/formal 'your'; بود is again copula 'would be/were' (here marking the apodosis/result of the conditional); آبروی means 'reputation/honor' and is the predicate: 'your reputation would be (kept) near me.'
segments.s061-g25: ز + پروازش is a poetic fused prepositional phrase meaning “from its flight.” آورد is the past tense ‘brought/caused’; here it takes گردان فرود as its object/complement, so the verb phrase means “caused/made a revolving (گردان) descent (فرود).” Word order is topical: cause/agent (from its flight) → verb → descriptive result (a circling/alighting).
segments.s061-g26: چکان here expresses dripping/letting fall (blood), followed by خون (blood) and the coordinating و. شده is the perfect/passive participle ‘became’ and governs آب رود “the river’s water.” وشی is a poetic quality-word modifying how the water became (i.e., a manner/quality — blood-stained); overall: “blood dripping, and the river’s water became (in that manner).”
segments.s061-g27: بترک here should be read as the fused phrase به ترک (“to the Turk”) — a common poetic run‑together of به + ترک. آنگهی “thereupon/then” introduces the speech; گفت is the reporting verb “said”; زان سو گذر is an instruction ‘pass on that side’ (زان = ‘that’, سو = ‘side/toward’, گذر = passage/pass).
segments.s061-g28: بیاور is the imperative ‘bring’; تو is the vocative/subject ‘you’; آن مرغ is ‘that bird’; افگنده پر is an adjectival phrase meaning ‘with drooping/scattered wings’ (افگنده modifying پر). The whole is a direct command: “You bring that bird whose wings are thrown/drooped.”
segments.s061-g29: به کشتی گذر کرد is a verbal phrase: به کشتی ‘by/with a boat’, گذر کردن ‘to pass/cross’ with کرد as the past-tense auxiliary = ‘crossed by ship.’ ترک سترگ is an appositional noun phrase identifying the subject as ‘the great/mighty Turk’ (poetic epithet).
segments.s061-g30: خرامید is the past tense of خرامیدن ‘to stride/strut proudly’; نزد governs the following noun phrase and means ‘toward/in the presence of’; پرستنده ترک is the object phrase ‘the worshipper (or admirer) Turk’ — so: ‘he proudly strode up to the worshipper.’
segments.s061-g31: پرستنده پرسید is straightforward past narration ‘the worshipper asked’; کای is a vocative particle ‘O’ addressing someone; پهلوان is the addressee ‘hero/champion.’ The sequence is ‘the worshipper asked, “O hero …”’
segments.s061-g32: سخن گوی is an exhortation: سخن (speech) plus گوی (here functioning as an imperative/command ‘speak’ or as ‘sayer’ — contextually an instruction to speak). و joins the next imperative بگشای ‘open/reveal’; شیرین زبان is an epithet ‘sweet‑tongued’ (literally ‘sweet tongue’) describing the speech or the addressee. Together: ‘Speak and unfold, O sweet‑tongued one, your words.’
segments.s061-g73: This clause is first-person singular future/intention expressed with the verb کنَم (کنم). کزین (کز + این) is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from this'. The noun phrase خایه‌گر ('scoundrel, literally “khāye‑maker”') is the object/source, and مایه بیرون کنَم is a light-verb construction where بیرون (out) plus کنَم means 'I will take out / pull out the substance'. Word order is preposition + object + result phrase.
segments.s061-g74: ز پشت پدر is a locative phrase 'from behind (the) father' where ز = 'from' and پشت = 'behind/back'. خایه (testicle) + بیرون کنم repeats the action 'I will take out the testicle'; کنم again marks the speaker's action. The phrase therefore specifies the bodily place/source of what is to be pulled out. Word order places the locative phrase before the repeated verb phrase for emphasis.
segments.s061-g75: ازیشان is the fused poetic form of از ایشان 'from them'. چو برگشت is a temporal clause 'when he returned' (چو = when; برگشت = returned). خندان غلام is the subject noun phrase 'the smiling servant/slave' placed after the temporal clause; the whole segment sets the time and agent for the following action.
segments.s061-g76: بپرسید is a past-tense narrative verb 'he asked'. از governs the noun phrase indicating what he inquired about ('about/from him'). و here functions as a coordinating/linking particle leading into the reported identification; نامور پور سام is reported speech/presented answer 'Namvar, son of Sam', with پور as a patronymic 'son of'. The segment packs question + reply into one line typical of epic narration.
segments.s061-g77: که introduces a relative/interrogative clause here: 'the one who...'. با تو is the dative-like phrase 'to you' (lit. 'with you' used as 'to/with you'). چه گفت is the question 'what did (he) say' with چه (what) before the verb گفت (said). آن‌که خندان شدی is a relative clause 'the one by whom you became smiling' — خندان شدی ('you became smiling') is a causative/resultive description meaning 'who made you laugh/smile'.
segments.s061-g78: گشاده لب و سیم دندان is a two-part adjectival description: گشاده لب 'open-lipped' (wide-mouthed, i.e., smiling) and سیم دندان literally 'silver-toothed' (bright/showing teeth). شدی is the second-person past 'you became' — together the phrase describes the change in the addressee's expression that prompted the question.
segments.s061-g79: بگفت is the past narrative verb 'he said / he told'. آنچه بشنید is a nominal content clause 'that which he heard' (آنچه = what/that which; بشنید = he heard). با پهلوان marks the indirect object 'to the champion/hero' (with/to the hero). The clause therefore reports the servant reporting speech to the hero.
segments.s061-g80: ز شادی is an ablative causal phrase 'from/with joy'. دل پهلوان is a possessive word order 'the heart of the champion' (owner after the possessed noun). شد جوان is the verbal phrase 'became young' (شد = became; جوان = young), a poetic way to say his heart was made fresh/vigorous by joy.
segments.s061-g97: مگر introduces a concessive clause here meaning 'unless' or 'except that'. آنکه is a relative/subordinating pronoun 'that/which/he who' linking the concession to the following verb. باشد is the classical subjunctive/optative form 'be' (here ‘there be’); میان دو تن is a prepositional phrase 'between two people' with the preposition میان before the numeral + noun.
segments.s061-g98: سه تن = 'three persons'. نانهانست is an older/poetic negative verbal/adjectival form meaning 'is not hidden' or 'nothing is kept secret' (نه + نهانست); و is the simple conjunction 'and'; چار انجمن = 'four (constitute) an assembly' (چار = four, انجمن = assembly/meeting). The line states a proverb-like observation about secrecy and company size.
segments.s061-g99: بگوی is an imperative/hortative form 'speak' (classical/poetic); ای marks the vocative 'O'; خردمند پاکیزه رای is a descriptive vocative phrase: خردمند 'wise', پاکیزه 'pure/clean' modifying رای 'counsel/opinion' — together 'O wise one of pure counsel'. Word order is vocative after the verb, typical in poetic address.
segments.s061-g100: سخن‌گر is an agent noun 'speaker/orator' (reinforcing the addressee). به رازست is a verbless or copular clause meaning 'is secret' or 'is in secret' (به + راز + ست 'is secret'); با ما سرای is an imperative phrase 'sing/utter/compose with us' (سرای here used poetically = 'speak/perform'); the sequence invites private speech with the group (با ما = 'with us').
segments.s061-g101: پرستنده is the plural/collective subject 'the worshippers/devotees'. گفتند is the past-tense verb 'they said/spoke' (third person plural); یک با دگر is an idiomatic phrase 'one with another' = 'to one another' (reciprocal communication). The order subject + verb + adverbial/object is standard in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s061-g102: که introduces the reported clause 'that ...'. آمد is simple past 'came/entered'. به دام اندرون is a compound locative 'into the inner trap/within the enclosure' (به + دام + اندرون). شیر نر is a noun phrase with modifier after the noun: شیر 'lion' + نر 'male' = 'male lion'.
segments.s061-g103: کنون = 'now' (temporal adverb). کار رودابه uses noun + proper name to mean 'the matter/affair of Rudabeh' (classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe but the possession is understood). و کام زال parallels that construction: کام 'desire' (here meaning wish/consummation) of زال (Zāl). The phrase sets up what has just been accomplished.
segments.s061-g104: به جای آمد is an idiom meaning 'came into place' = 'came to pass / was fulfilled' (به + جای + آمد). و این بود is 'and this was' (بود as past copula). نیک فال = 'good/auspicious omen' (نیک here 'auspicious' modifying فال 'omen'). The clause reports the favorable outcome.
segments.s061-g89: 'نباید' is an impersonal modal meaning 'should not / must not' and governs the following verbal idea. 'شدن' here functions as the verbal infinitive/participial idea of 'becoming/going' (the action that ought not occur). 'شان' is the possessive 'their', and 'سوی کاخ باز' is a directional phrase 'toward the palace again' (سوی = toward; کاخ = palace; باز = again). The whole segment expresses: 'It ought not to be that they return toward the palace.'
segments.s061-g90: بدان here is written as two pieces (ب + دان) in the word list; treat ب as a prepositional prefix 'to/into' and دان as the root 'to know/consider' forming a deictic sense ('there/so that they know') in context. 'تا' introduces a purpose/temporal clause meaning 'until / so that', and 'پیامی فرستم' is a subjunctive/future sense 'I send a message' (فرستم = 'I send / I shall send'). 'براز' is unclear in meaning in this context (it may be a proper name or an obscure term); the clause as a whole means 'wait there until I send a message.'
segments.s061-g91: 'برفتند' is the simple past plural 'they went / departed'. 'زی' is a poetic genitive/partitive 'of / from' linking to the following descriptive noun phrase. 'ماه رخسار' is a compound epithet 'moon‑cheeked / moon‑faced' (ماه = moon, رخسار = cheek / countenance). The numeral 'پنج' (placed after the noun in this order) quantifies them: 'five moon‑faced ones departed.'
segments.s061-g92: 'ابا' here denotes reluctance/refusal (used nominally: 'with reluctance'). 'گرم گفتار' is a collocation 'warm / fervent speech' (گرم modifying گفتار). The series 'و دینار و گنج' lists what accompanies them — 'and dinars and treasure' — with و functioning as the coordinating 'and'. The whole segment describes the manner and goods accompanying their departure.
segments.s061-g93: 'بدیشان' is a prepositional/enclitic form meaning 'to them / for them'. 'سپردند' is past plural 'they entrusted / handed over'. 'زر و گهر' are the direct objects 'gold and gem(s) / jewels'. The clause is a straightforward ditransitive: 'They handed them gold and jewels.'
segments.s061-g94: 'پیام' is 'message' or 'tidings'. The following nominal group 'جهان‌پهلوان زال' names the addressee/appellant 'the world‑champion, Zal' (جهان‌پهلوان = world‑champion; زال = the hero's name). The final 'زر' (gold) states what the message/offer was or what was given along with the message. Read together as: 'a message for the world‑champion Zal — (it was) gold.'
segments.s061-g95: 'پرستنده' is an agent noun meaning 'worshipper / admirer / devoted follower' (here: the lover/suitor). 'با ماه دیدار' is a prepositional phrase 'in meeting with the moon' (با = with; ماه = the moon / the moon‑faced beloved; دیدار = meeting). 'گفت' here introduces speech ('said / spoke') — the verb that leads into the reported clause.
segments.s061-g96: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. 'هرگز' is the negative adverb 'never'. 'نماند' is a present/subjunctive form of 'ماندن' meaning 'remain / be left' (used here as 'will not remain' / 'shall not be left'). 'سخن در نهفت' is the collocation 'speech/word hidden' (سخن = word/speech; در نهفت = hidden/kept within). Together: 'that never would any word remain hidden.'
segments.s061-g49: به بالای ساج است — a prepositional phrase plus copula: به بالای (to/on the top of) + ساج (the place/object) and then the copula است 'is/stands'. Word order places the locative phrase before the copula. همرنگ عاج is a coordinating adjectival phrase (و + adjective + noun) that comments on the same subject: همرنگ (same-colored) modifying عاج (ivory).
segments.s061-g50: یکی ایزدی بر سر از مشک تاج — یکی (an indefinite 'one'/'a certain one') + ایزدی (adjective 'divine') forms the NP 'a divine one'. بر سر is the locative 'on (the) head' (بر = on, سر = head). از here introduces the material/quality 'of' (a crown made/of musk): از مشک تاج = 'a crown of musk'. The whole clause attaches to the previous clause as the subject that 'is' described above.
segments.s061-g51: دو نرگس دژم و دو ابرو به خم — a coordinated list: دو نرگس دژم ('two narcissus [eyes], sullen') where نرگس is a poetic term for a beautiful eye and دژم is an adjective 'sullen/grim'. و دو ابرو introduces 'and two eyebrows'. به خم is a locative/phrasing 'in a curve' where به + خم characterizes the shape of the brows (preposition + noun used adverbially).
segments.s061-g52: ستون دو ابرو چو سیمین قلم — ستون here means 'pillar' or the upright/thick appearance formed by the two brows; دو ابرو repeats the referent. چو 'like/as' introduces a simile comparing that pillar to سیمین قلم ('silver pen' or a slender silver stylus). The simile structure is standard Persian: X چو Y = X like Y.
segments.s061-g53: دهانش به تنگی دل مستمند — دهانش is the possessed noun (دهان + enclitic ـش = 'her mouth'). به تنگی is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/with the narrowness' and modifies the verbless clause as a state ('mouth tight with...'). دل مستمند is a genitival phrase 'the needy/longing heart' (دل modified by مستمند). The whole segment is descriptive and lacks an explicit verb; Persian allows such verbless predicates.
segments.s061-g54: سر زلف چون حلقه پای‌بند — سر زلف = 'the head (or tip) of the lock of hair' (سر + زلف in a genitive-like link). چون introduces a simile: 'like a حلقه (a ring)'. پای‌بند is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'bound to the foot' or 'tied to the ankle' and here describes how that ring-like curl appears (tightly bound). The structure again is descriptive, comparing the hair's curl to a ring fastened to the foot.
segments.s061-g55: دو جادوش پر خواب و پرآب روی — دو جادوش is a poetic expression (جادو/جادوش used metaphorically as 'her two enchantments' or 'two features that enchant'); پر + noun is a common construction meaning 'full of' (پر خواب = 'full of sleep' = sleepy/heavy-eyed). و links to پرآب ('full of moisture' = moist/teary). روی (face) closes the clause and is the locus: 'on the face'. The adjectives follow the noun cluster in a verbless descriptive phrase.
segments.s061-g56: پر از لاله رخسار و پر مشک موی — two parallel 'پر' phrases: پر از لاله رخسار = 'face full of tulip (i.e. tulip‑red)'; here از introduces the source/contents ('full of'). و connects the second parallel: پر مشک موی = 'hair full of musk' (پر + noun where the noun names what it is full of). The order is adjective-like 'پر از X' following the head (face/hair) that is being described.
segments.s061-g129: دگر here functions as 'no other/another' in a comparative sense; چون means 'like' and links دگر to the following vocative. تو‌ای is the poetic vocative 'O you' (تو + ای) addressing the hero; پهلوان is the noun 'champion/hero' followed by the adjective دلیر 'brave' (Persian commonly places adjectives after the noun). The whole chunk reads 'no other like you, O brave champion.'
segments.s061-g130: بدین is a fused locative/comparative particle here ('in/with this' or 'by this means'), introducing the qualities that follow. برز بالا is a noun phrase meaning 'high/exalted stature' (برز 'height/exalted' + بالا 'lofty'). و links the second quality, بازوی شیر, where بازوی is 'arm' with the genitive شیر 'of a lion' (lion's arm = mighty arm). Word order is typical Persian: modifier after noun and genitive without an explicit ezāfe marking in script.
segments.s061-g131: همی is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning 'still/continually'; می‌چکد is the present habitual/continuous third-person singular verb 'it drips/trickles'. گویی here functions as the particle 'it seems/as if' (compare گویا) rather than a literal verb 'you say'. از روی تو is a prepositional phrase 'from/off your face' (از = 'from', روی = 'face', تو = 'your'). The clause reads 'It still seems to drip from your face.'
segments.s061-g132: عبیرست is a nominal/copular phrase meaning '(it) is perfume' or 'fragrant'; the following گویی repeats the evaluative 'it seems/as if'. مگر here is a rhetorical particle meaning 'perhaps / is it not that / maybe' introducing a conjecture, and بوی تو is literally 'your scent' (بوی = scent, تو = your). The line thus gives the impression 'It is perfume, it seems — perhaps (it is) your scent.'
segments.s061-g133: سه دیگر means 'three others' (سه = three; دیگر = other/another). چو is the simile particle 'like/as', introducing a comparison to رودابه (the proper name Rudabeh). ماه روی is a compound adjective 'moon-faced' (ماه 'moon' used metaphorically to describe روی 'face'). Word order shows the poetic compression common in Ferdowsi: numeral + 'other' + 'like' + name + descriptive compound.
segments.s061-g134: یکی 'one' introduces a singled-out member of the group. سرو is 'cypress' used as a conventional image for tall/slender beauty; سیمست (سیمست = سیم + ست) is a predicate 'is silver' or 'silver-hued' describing that cypress-like figure. با رنگ و بوی is a prepositional phrase 'with color (complexion) and scent' — with = با, رنگ 'complexion/colour', و 'and', بوی 'scent'. The adjective/attribute follows the noun as is typical.
segments.s061-g135: ز ... تا is the classical 'from ... to' construction: ز سر تا به پایش = 'from head to her foot' (ز = from, سر = head, تا = to/until, به پایش = to her foot; -ش is the possessive suffix 'her'). گلست is a copular/nominal phrase 'is a flower' (گل + است contracted), and وسمن adds 'and jasmine' (و + سمن). The clause thus means 'from head to foot she is flower and jasmine.'
segments.s061-g136: به here introduces a comparative/figurative likeness 'as/regarding' (literally 'to/for'); سرو سهی is a descriptive nominal 'a dark/black cypress' (سرو = cypress, سهی = a poetic epithet 'dark/black' or 'sable'). بر functions as 'over/above' or 'compared to', and سهیل یمن is a classical star-name + place ('Suhail of Yemen' — سهیل = Canopus/Suhail, یمن = Yemen). The whole phrase says she, as a dark cypress, stands (or outshines) Suhail of Yemen. This is a compact poetic comparative construction.
segments.s061-g121: وگر is the conditional 'if' introducing a hypothetical. هیچ کژی is a noun phrase meaning 'any wrongdoing/irregularity' (هیچ here functioning as 'any/no' in a conditional). گمانی is 'a suspicion/turn of thought'. برم is first‑person singular (optative/subjunctive) from بردن used idiomatically as 'take/harbour (a thought)' — so together the clause means 'if I were to harbour any suspicion.'
segments.s061-g122: به + زیر + پی + پیلتان is a prepositional phrase: به 'to/into', زیر 'under', پی 'foot/tread' (here 'the foot/tread of'), پیلتان 'your elephant' (پیلتان = پیل + -تان). بسپرم is first‑person singular subjunctive/optative of سپردن 'to entrust/consign', used as the consequent of the conditional: 'I would consign (it) under the elephant's foot.'
segments.s061-g123: This is a compact poetic clause: رخ لاله رخ گشت literally 'face (became) tulip‑face became'. گشت is the past tense 'became/turned'. چون introduces a simile 'like', and سندروس (a fragrant wood) follows as the object of comparison. The sequence describes the face changing to a tulip‑like color/appearance, compared to sandalwood.
segments.s061-g124: به پیش + سپهبد is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before the spahbed' (به 'to/at', پیش 'before/in the presence of'). زمین is the direct object 'earth/ground'. داد + بوس is a periphrastic expression 'gave a kiss' (داد 'gave', بوس here as noun 'a kiss'), i.e. 'kissed the ground.' Word order is archaic/poetic but straightforward: 'she kissed the ground before the spahbed.'
segments.s061-g125: چنین گفت is an introductory formula 'she said thus' (چنین 'thus', گفت here functioning as the verb 'said' in poetic diction). کز 'that/from which' introduces a subordinate clause; مادر 'mother', اندر جهان 'in the world' give the setting for what follows. The clause is reporting speech.
segments.s061-g126: نزاید is the present/present-habitual form 'is born/comes into being' (from زادن). کس 'anyone' (used here in a negative sense with the context), اندر میان 'in the midst of/among', مهان 'guests'. The clause asserts that, in the world, no one is born (or exists) among the guests from a mother — a compact, somewhat elliptical poetic statement about origins or uniqueness.
segments.s061-g127: به دیدار سام is 'in the sight/meeting of Sam' (به marking direction/relatum). The coordination و + به بالای او adds 'and before his high station/height' (بالای او = his loftiness/position). These are locative/relational phrases setting where or in whose presence the following comment applies.
segments.s061-g128: به here introduces the grounds or respect: 'because of / with regard to'. پاکی دل 'the purity of (his) heart', دانش 'learning/knowledge', و رای او 'and his counsel/opinion' — a list of qualities. او (= his) refers back to Sam; the whole segment gives the reasons or virtues considered.
segments.s061-g65: ولیکن is a conjunction meaning 'but' or 'however' at the start of the clause. به گفتن is a prepositional/infinitival phrase ('in saying' / 'to say') with به + verbal noun گفتن; this frames the action. مگر روی نیست is idiomatic: مگر 'except/unless' here supports a negative statement and روی 'face' literally but idiomatically means 'occasion, courage, or standing to say (something)'; نیست negates it ('there is no...').
segments.s061-g66: بود is the past copular/existential 'there was'. کاب را uses را as a topic/object marker, marking 'kāb' ('nightmare/affliction') as the item being spoken about. ره is 'way/path'. بدین is the prepositional phrase 'to/into this' (here split in the tokenization as ب + دین; the second part does not match the senses provided and functions as part of the demonstrative 'this'). جوی 'stream/course' and نیست negates the whole: 'there was no way for the nightmare along this course.'
segments.s061-g67: دلاور is the subject 'the brave/heroic man'. که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' linking to the following verb. پرهیز جوید is a verb phrase: پرهیز 'abstinence/caution' is the object/noun and جوید (from جستن/جو) is the present-tense verb 'seeks/searches for' — together 'who seeks abstinence'. ز جفت is a prepositional phrase 'from (his) pair/mate' (ز = from, جفت = mate/spouse).
segments.s061-g68: بماند is a form of ماندن ('remain') with the prefix ب-; here it expresses general/habitual result 'will remain' or 'remains'. به آسانی is an adverbial phrase 'with ease / easily' (به + آسانی). اندر نهفت is a locative/descriptive phrase: اندر 'in/within' and نهفت 'hidden/concealed' (participle/adjective), together 'hidden within' or simply 'concealed'.
segments.s061-g69: بدان تاش here functions as a purposive phrase 'by/for that effort' (بدان = 'to/for that', تاش = 'effort/attempt'). دختر نباشد is a negative jussive/optative: دختر 'daughter/maiden' + نباشد ('let there not be' / 'should not be'). ز بن is a prepositional phrase 'from the root/foundation' (ز = from, بن = root/base), indicating origin.
segments.s061-g70: نباید is the impersonal prohibition 'one should not / it must not'. شنیدنش is the verbal noun شنیدن 'hearing' with the enclitic ش ('it/him/her'), so 'hearing it'. ننگ سخن is a genitive construction 'the shame of speech' (ننگ = shame, سخن = speech/words), i.e. 'shameful words'. The clause says that such shameful words ought not to be heard.
segments.s061-g71: چنین 'thus' introduces reported speech. گفت here is the verb 'said' introducing the quotation; مر is the dative 'to me' (i.e. 'he said to me'). جفت را marks 'the pair/mate' with را as topic/object marker. باز نر is an appositive noun phrase 'the male hawk' (باز = 'hawk/falcon' here, نر = 'male'), describing that male.
segments.s061-g72: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بر خایه بنشست is a prepositional phrase + verb: بر 'on/upon' + خایه 'testicle' + بنشست (past of نشستن) 'sat down'. و connects the two verbs, گسترد پر = 'spread (گسترد) the wing/feather (پر)' — a simple past sequence describing the hawk-like action.
segments.s061-g81: چنین گفت is a formulaic clause 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so' + گفت = 'said'); با introduces the addressee. ریدک is here a proper name (or epithet) and ماه روی is an epithet apposed to that name (ماه = 'moon' used metaphorically for beauty, روی = 'face'), so با ریدک ماه روی = 'to Rīdak, moon‑faced.' Word order places the epithet after the name in poetic register.
segments.s061-g82: که introduces reported speech ('that' / 'namely'); رو here is an imperative verb 'come/turn' (poetic imperative of رَوَیدن/رَوَ) taking the enclitic مر 'to me' (مر = 'to me'), so رو مر = 'come to me.' پرستندگان را is the definite object 'the worshippers' (را marks the object). بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive 'tell' reporting the command: 'Tell the worshippers to come to me.'
segments.s061-g83: که again introduces a subordinate clause. از گلستان = 'from/through the rose‑garden' (از + place). یک زمان is an adverbial phrase 'for a while / once' (literally 'one time'). مگذرید is a verbal form meaning 'pass/spend (time) / go through' in this poetic context (here used as an instruction to pass through); the exact morphological shape is archaic/poetic.
segments.s061-g84: مگر introduces an exception/condition 'unless.' با گل = 'with a flower'; از باغ = 'from the garden' (source). گوهر is 'jewel/gem' as the thing taken from the garden; برید is the verb 'take/carry' (imperative/subjunctive/inflected form here) meaning 'bring/take.' The phrase gives the exception: 'unless they take a jewel from the garden together with a flower.'
segments.s061-g85: درم خواست is a simple past clause 'he requested dirhams' (درم = dirham, خواست = 'wanted/asked for'). The objects are enumerated with و: 'and dinars and gems.' ز گنج indicates source/location 'from the treasure/treasury.' Word order places the requested items before the source prepositional phrase in this poetic line.
segments.s061-g86: گرانمایه is an adjective 'precious/costly' qualifying the noun phrase. دیبای زربفت is a compound noun phrase 'gold‑woven brocade' (دیبا = brocade/finely woven silk, زربفت = gold‑woven/gilt). پنج at the end (not listed among the words here) is the numeral 'five' modifying the whole phrase; the adjective–noun–modifier order is a poetic inversion of the more prosaic word order.
segments.s061-g87: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/said' (polite past). کاین = 'that these' introduces the content of the order. نزد ایشان = 'to/into their presence' (نزد = 'in the presence of/near', ایشان = 'them' as honorific plural). برید is a verbal form 'be brought / be taken' (poetic imperative/subjunctive) meaning the items should be carried to them.
segments.s061-g88: کسی را مگوئید is a negative injunction 'tell no one' (کسی = 'anyone/someone', را marks the object, مگوئید = 'do not tell'). و پنهان برید connects a second command: پنهان = 'hidden' used as an imperative/verb 'hide' in context, and برید again functions as 'carry/hide (them)'—together: 'and hide them.' The coordination gives two linked orders.
segments.s061-g137: از آن گنبد سیم سر بر زمین is a compact descriptive phrase: از آن = 'from that'; گنبد سیم = 'dome of silver' (a poetic image for a shining head or crown); سر بر زمین places 'head' with بر زمین ('on/upon the earth' or 'down to the ground'). Word order is poetic (modifier after noun), and سر is the anatomical 'head' referring back to the person described.
segments.s061-g138: فرو هشته بر گل کمند از کمین strings together adverbial and nominal images: فرو 'down(ward)' modifies هشته 'a lock/tuft (of hair)'; بر گل = 'upon the flower(s)'; کمند 'a snare/lasso' is used metaphorically; از کمین 'from ambush' qualifies the metaphor, i.e. the hanging lock lies over the flowers as if a snare sprung from hiding. There is no overt copula—classical Persian often stacks images this way.
segments.s061-g139: به مشک و به عنبر سرش بافته = 'with musk and with amber her head is woven/braided.' The repeated به marks the instrument/material ('with'); سرش = 'her head' (سر + enclitic -ش 'her'); بافته is a past participle 'woven/knit/braided' used predicatively to mean 'braided/embroidered with'.
segments.s061-g140: به یاقوت و زمرد تنش تافته = 'with ruby and emerald her body (تنش) is tAfteh.' Again به introduces the materials; تنش = 'her body' (تن + enclitic -ش); تافته is a participle/adjective here meaning 'woven/ornamented' or 'radiant/embroidered' — the phrase says her body was as if woven of rubies and emeralds.
segments.s061-g141: سر زلف و جعدش چو مشکین زره = 'the head of locks and her curls, like a musk-dark armor/coat.' سر here heads the phrase ('the head of the locks'), زلف = 'lock(s) of hair', جعدش = 'its/ her curl(s)' (جعد + -ش), چو = 'like/as', مشکین = 'musk-colored / dark', زره = 'armor' (a conventional poetic simile comparing glossy dark hair to armor).
segments.s061-g142: فگندست گویی گره بر گره is a compact, slightly elliptical clause: the verb فگندست (classical/poetic) is the action attributed to the hair (here glossed 'fell/was cast/laid'); گویی functions as 'as if'/'one would say' (classical usage meaning 'it seemed'); گره بر گره = 'knot upon knot' (repetition intensifies the image). The overall sense: the curls lay arranged so densely they appeared as knot upon knot.
segments.s061-g143: ده انگشت برسان سیمین قلم = a noun phrase where ده انگشت برسان qualifies قلم: literally 'a silvery pen reaching (برسان) ten fingers.' ده انگشت is a size measure ('ten fingers'), برسان here conveys 'reaching up to'/'measuring out to' (classical idiom for length), سیمین = 'silvery' modifies قلم = 'pen/stylus'.
segments.s061-g144: برو کرده از غالیه صدرقم = 'it had been made from costly/precious things (of the most excellent rank).' کرده is the past participle 'made/done'; از غالیه = 'from precious/expensive (materials)'; صدرقم is a poetic qualifier meaning something like 'foremost/excellent' (here qualifying the precious material). The exact reading of برو is contextually idiomatic/archaic and here helps form the compound verbal phrase with کرده ('had been ...'), i.e. 'had been wrought/made'.
segments.s061-g145: بت آرای functions as a poetic compound referring to a superb beauty (literally 'idol-beauty' or 'beauty that would adorn idols'). چون او نبیند is a conditional/subordinate clause: چون = 'if/when/like', او = 'her/him', نبیند is third-person present (negative) meaning 'does not see' (classical/poetic present). بچین is an imperative 'pluck/gather/choose!' commanding action; it connects as the main imperative triggered by the conditional idea.
segments.s061-g146: برو is an imperative 'go' or a hortatory/commandive particle here introducing the action; ماه و پروین are the compound subject 'the Moon and the Pleiades' (a conventional pair of heavenly beauties). کنند is third-person plural present/subjunctive 'they do/make' used here to mean 'they would utter/make', and آفرین is the noun/exclamation 'praise, bravo' — together 'they would exclaim "bravo".'
segments.s061-g147: Word order is Subject (سپهبد) + indirect object/topic (پرستنده را) + verb (گفت) + manner adverb/adjective (گرم). گفت here is the past tense 'said' (classical narrative), and گرم modifies the speech (meaning 'warmly' or 'fervently'). The را after پرستنده marks the addressee/definite object.
segments.s061-g148: سخنهای شیرین is a noun phrase 'sweet words/speeches' (سخنهای = plural/construct). به آوای نرم is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in/with a soft voice': به used adverbially ('by/with'), آوا = 'voice/melody', نرم = 'soft/gentle' describing the manner of speaking.
segments.s061-g149: که introduces an indirect/question clause ('so/that'); اکنون = 'now'. چه چارست is an idiomatic/classical phrase meaning 'what is the matter/what remedy is there' (چار here is an archaic word for 'remedy' or 'what to do'), and با من بگوی is an imperative request 'tell me' (بگوی is imperative/optative 'say/tell').
segments.s061-g150: یکی راه = 'one way' or 'a single method'; جستن is the infinitive 'to seek/attempt' used here nominally ('a way of seeking' = 'a way to do X'). به نزدیک اوی = 'to (come) near him/her' — به marks direction/purpose, نزدیک = 'near', and اوی is an older/pronounced form of the third-person pronoun referring back to the beloved.
segments.s061-g151: که marks the clause 'that'. ما را places 'us' as the affected party (object/topic). دل و جان is a parallel compound 'heart and life/soul' serving as the internal subject. پر از مهر اوست is the predicate: پر = 'full (of)', از introduces the content/source ('of/by'), مهر = 'love/affection', اوست = 'is his/her' (copula with the pronoun).
segments.s061-g152: همه آرزو = 'all (our) desire/longing' (همه emphasizes totality). دیدن is the infinitive 'to see' functioning as a noun (the thing desired), چهر او = 'his/her face'. ست is the copula 'is' — together: 'All (we) desire is to see his/her face.'
segments.s061-g153: پرستنده گفتا is an archaic past-reporting phrase: گرفت 'گفتا' = 'said'. چو here introduces the conditional ('if' / 'when') and governs the subordinate clause فرمان دهی ('you give the command'); دهی is a present/subjunctive 2nd-person form meaning 'you give' or 'you order'.
segments.s061-g154: گذاریم is 1st‑person plural ('we will let / we allow') used here as a future/intent. تا marks the limit or goal ('up to / into' the place) and so links to کاخ 'palace'; سرو سهی is a descriptive compound ('cypress' + poetic epithet) describing that palace.
segments.s061-g155: ز is the poetic preposition 'by / from'; فرخنده رای is a noun + adjective phrase meaning 'auspicious counsel' or 'fortunate plan'; جهان‌پهلوان is an epithet apposed to رای ('the hero / world‑champion') and identifies whose counsel it is.
segments.s061-g156: This repeats ز to govern the following coordinated nouns: گفتار و دیدار 'speech and meeting/appearance'. روشن‌روان is an epithet ('bright‑souled' / cheerful) modifying the person whose words and appearance are meant. The whole segment functions in parallel to the previous one ('by X and by Y').
segments.s061-g157: فریبیم و گوییم are first‑person plural present forms ('we will charm/entice and we say'); here they express intent/plan ('we'll flatter and say ...'). هر گونه‌ای means 'any kind / any manner' and modifies what they will say. (Note: the listed dictionary senses for گوییم are noun senses; the verbal 'گوییم' functions as 'we say'.)
segments.s061-g158: میان اندرون literally 'in the midst of the inner place' (میان = middle/among, اندرون = inside/inner apartments). نیست negates the predicate: 'there is not' and واژونه‌ای ('upside‑down / reversed / amiss') is the predicate noun/adjective — 'there is nothing amiss inside.'
segments.s061-g159: سرمشک بویش is a genitive phrase: سرمشک (musk) modifies بو 'scent', with the enclitic یش = 'her/its' attached to بو (بویش = 'her scent'). به دام آوریم is an idiom 'to bring into a trap / to ensnare'; آوریم is 1st‑person plural 'we bring / we cause'.
segments.s061-g160: لبش 'her/his lip' (possessive enclitic). زی is the archaic/prepositional form 'زِ/از' = 'from / of', so لبش زی لب پور سام = 'her lip from the lip of Pour‑Sam' (پور سام = 'Pour‑Sam', a name built from پور 'son' + سام). آوریم again 'we bring' — 'we will take/bring her lips from Pour‑Sam's lips.'
segments.s061-g33: که introduces the reported question/clause ('that/which/who'); این is the demonstrative 'this'. شیر بازو is an epithetic compound: شیر ('lion') + بازو ('arm') = 'lion‑armed' (an epithet describing great strength). گو here is the adjectival/epithetic element (‑go, literally '‑speaking') used in compounds to form an epithet, and پیلتن is a proper name/place in apposition to that epithet.
segments.s061-g34: چه مردست is an interrogative clause: چه = 'what/what kind of' and مردست is a contracted poetic spelling of مرد است ('is a man' / 'is a person'); و = 'and' links the two questions; شاه کدام انجمن asks 'the king of which assembly/which court is he' with کدام = 'which' and انجمن = 'assembly/court'.
segments.s061-g35: که again introduces the subordinate (reported) clause. بگشاد is an archaic poetic past/punctiliar form with the prefix بـ meaning 'loosed/opened' (here 'loosed' an arrow). زین گونه = 'of this kind/this sort' (زین is an older demonstrative 'this' and گونه = 'kind/manner'); تیر از کمان = 'an arrow from (the) bow' with از indicating source ('from').
segments.s061-g36: چه is the interrogative 'what' (here introducing a rhetorical question). سنجد in the line is not the noun 'oleaster' but an archaic poetic verb form (سَنجَد) meaning '(can) weigh/compare' (3rd person); به پیش اندرش = 'before him / in his presence' (به پیش = 'before/in front of', اندرش = 'in/within him' or 'toward him'); بدگمان is a predicate/adjective 'suspicious' — the clause says people view him with doubt/suspicion. (Note: the written form coincides with the fruit-name, but context shows a verbal sense.)
segments.s061-g37: ندیدیم is the first‑person plural past negative 'we did not see' (used here as a generalizing past: 'we have not seen'). زیبنده‌تر is the comparative 'more fitting/more comely'. زین سوار = 'this rider' (زین an older demonstrative 'this' + سوار 'rider'), so the whole segment: 'we have seen no rider more fitting than this.'
segments.s061-g38: به تیر و کمان is instrumental/locative: 'with bow and arrow' (به here marks the domain/means). بر چنين کامگار = 'so successful/skillful' (بر is a prepositional particle here linking to کامگار, and چنین = 'such/so'); together the phrase qualifies the rider as unusually successful with bow and arrow.
segments.s061-g39: پری روی is a compound epithet: پری ('a beautiful woman' / 'fairy') + روی ('face') = 'fair‑faced' or 'the fair one'. دندان به لب برنهاد is a short poetic sequence: دندان به لب ('tooth to the lip') + برنهاد (past 'placed/put') — an idiom here meaning she showed a faint smile or put her teeth against her lip (a discreet smile).
segments.s061-g40: مکن is the prohibitive imperative 'do not'. گفت here functions as 'saying/speech' (the poetic line uses گفت as the act of speaking; in prose one would expect a verb 'گو'/'گفت' as 'said'); ازین گونه = 'in this way/thus' (از + ین = از این 'of/from this' with ین as orthographic variant of این; گونه = 'manner'). از شاه یاد = 'mention of the king' or 'speak of the king' (یاد = 'remembrance/mention'). Together: 'Do not speak of the king in this manner,' she (thus) said.
